rubric_identity.one_sentence_purpose: Evaluate CVs for production-focused AI platform architect and player-coach roles that require architecture ownership, delivery proof, reliability discipline, and stakeholder translation rather than formal executive leadership.
rubric_identity.core_persona: Architect-first player-coach who turns AI concepts into production platforms with evaluation, reliability, and governance controls, while mentoring teams and translating across engineering and business stakeholders without overstating org ownership.
job_overlay_notes: Despite the Head of AI label, this market behaves like senior IC or player-coach scope (86.8% senior_ic, 31.6% player_coach, only 5.3% manager/director each)
Platform design is near-universal (94.7%) while hiring (5.3%), org-building (10.5%), and budget/P&L (0%) are rare
Reward delivery, reliability, evaluation, and governance proof over research framing or executive claims
No single technical stack dominates — Python leads at only 36.8%, with AWS and Kubernetes at 23.7% each

what_good_looks_like: Strongest 4-6 keywords appear 2-3 times across headline/profile/experience/skills: platform architecture, system design, production AI, reliability, evaluation, collaboration
Secondary 4-6 keywords appear 1-2 times: guardrails, governance, mentoring, observability, delivery, scalability
Architecture-forward headline without executive inflation: AI Architect, Principal Engineer AI Platform, Technical Lead AI Systems
Stack keywords used only when true and not overfit to one language — no single hard skill reaches 60% in this category
common_failures: Keyword stuffing with executive titles not supported by evidence (Head of AI, VP of AI, Chief AI Officer)
Over-indexing on one stack when the category is fragmented (Python 36.8%, AWS 23.7%, Kubernetes 23.7%)
Using research-heavy framing when delivery and production proof matter more (research_heavy only 10.5%)
Missing table-stakes keywords: platform architecture, reliability, delivery, collaboration

what_to_adjust_per_jd: If JD emphasizes specific cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP), increase that stack's keyword density to 2-3 mentions
If JD mentions specific AI frameworks (LangChain, LangGraph, RAG), ensure 1-2 mentions if truthfully applicable
If JD emphasizes observability tools (Datadog, Prometheus), include specific tool names once if true
company_domain_overrides: Consulting roles (13.2%): emphasize stakeholder translation, enterprise integration keywords
Enterprise SaaS (10.5%): emphasize API integration, scalability, multi-tenant architecture
Cybersecurity/Fintech (7.9% each): only add compliance keywords (GDPR, PCI) if explicitly evidenced
region_overrides: EEA region does not require native language keywords — remote_first is 50% of roles
Do not add GDPR/compliance keywords unless directly evidenced in candidate record

9_10: All table-stakes keywords present with appropriate density (architecture, platform, delivery, reliability, collaboration)
Headline uses safe title family (Architect, Principal/Staff Engineer, Technical Lead) without executive inflation
ATS-friendly formatting with clear section headers and consistent keyword placement
Stack keywords match candidate evidence and appear at appropriate density without overfit
7_8: Most table-stakes keywords present; one or two differentiator keywords missing
Headline is architecture-forward but may lack production AI specificity
Minor keyword density imbalances — strongest keywords appear 1-2 times instead of 2-3
5_6: Table-stakes keywords present but buried or appearing only once
Headline is generic (Software Engineer, Engineering Lead) without architecture or AI platform signal
Stack keywords not aligned with candidate evidence or overfit to one technology
0_4: Missing multiple table-stakes keywords (architecture, delivery, reliability)
Headline uses executive titles unsupported by evidence (Head of AI, VP, Director)
Keyword stuffing with irrelevant terms or fabricated technical skills
Research-heavy framing when candidate evidence is production-focused

red_flags: Executive title keywords (Head of AI, VP, Chief AI Officer) without evidence of formal org leadership
PhD or publication keywords when candidate has no research credentials
Full MLOps or custom LLM training keywords when evidence shows integration-level work
Compliance/governance keywords (GDPR, PCI) without explicit evidence of that ownership

what_good_looks_like: Architecture achievements quantified with delivery outcomes: systems launched, adoption metrics, latency/throughput/uptime improvements, deployment frequency
Reliability proof with operational metrics: incident rate reduction, MTTR, SLO attainment, error budget, alert volume reduction
Evaluation and quality achievements: quality score improvement, hallucination/error reduction, acceptance rate, manual review reduction
Delivery acceleration metrics: cycle time, lead time, PR turnaround, defect escape rate, team throughput, automation coverage
Mentoring impact without overstating org scope: engineers mentored, promotions influenced, design reviews led
common_failures: Vague architecture claims without delivery proof (designed platform with no adoption or scale metrics)
Reliability claims without operational metrics (improved observability with no MTTR or incident data)
AI claims without evaluation or quality metrics (built RAG system with no retrieval quality or user adoption data)
Leadership claims without influence proof (mentored team with no promotions or skill development outcomes)
Business metrics claimed without clear ownership link (revenue growth without showing architectural contribution)

what_to_adjust_per_jd: If JD emphasizes scale, foreground adoption metrics (users, tenants, throughput, request volume)
If JD emphasizes reliability/SRE, foreground operational metrics (uptime, MTTR, incident reduction)
If JD emphasizes AI quality, foreground evaluation metrics (MRR, NDCG, hallucination rate, acceptance rate)
company_domain_overrides: Consulting roles: emphasize client delivery timelines, integration success rates, partner adoption
Enterprise SaaS: emphasize multi-tenant scale, customer onboarding time, API reliability
Cybersecurity/Fintech: emphasize compliance audit outcomes, security incident metrics if evidenced
region_overrides: Remote-first roles (50%): include metrics on distributed team delivery, async collaboration efficiency
EEA-specific compliance outcomes only if directly evidenced in candidate record

9_10: Every major achievement has specific, verifiable metrics tied to architecture, delivery, or reliability outcomes
Metrics are contextually meaningful (75% incident reduction, 3 years zero downtime, 2000 users)
Clear causal link between candidate actions and measured outcomes
AI-specific metrics where applicable (retrieval quality, evaluation scores, guardrail effectiveness)
7_8: Most achievements have metrics; one or two use qualitative impact language
Metrics are credible but some lack baseline or context for interpretation
Clear ownership of outcomes but some metrics are team-level rather than individual contribution
5_6: Some achievements have metrics but many rely on qualitative descriptions
Metrics present but vague (improved performance, reduced incidents) without specific numbers
Unclear ownership — metrics may reflect team or org outcomes rather than candidate contribution
0_4: Few or no quantified outcomes; achievements described in general terms
Metrics present but not credible (100% improvement, eliminated all incidents)
No clear link between candidate actions and claimed outcomes
Unsourced or fabricated metrics with no evidence basis

red_flags: Revenue or business metrics claimed without clear architectural ownership link
Team size or org scope metrics that inflate actual management responsibility
Round-number metrics (50%, 100%) that suggest estimation rather than measurement
AI model performance metrics (accuracy, F1) when candidate work was integration not training

what_good_looks_like: Platform architecture and system design evidence (table-stakes: 94.7% of roles require platform design)
Delivery and reliability proof showing production AI maturity (delivery 95.2%, reliability 81.0% in deep analyses)
Evaluation and guardrails evidence for trustworthy AI outputs (evaluation 52.4%, governance 47.6%)
Cross-functional collaboration and communication (collaboration 81.6%, communication 68.4%)
Mentoring and technical leadership without overstating people management (mentoring 47.4%, hiring only 5.3%)
common_failures: Executive leadership framing when category is senior IC/player-coach (manager only 5.3%, director 5.3%)
Research-centered positioning when delivery proof matters more (research_heavy only 10.5%)
People management claims without evidence (hiring 5.3%, performance management 7.9%, org_building 10.5%)
Missing architecture proof when it is the clearest anchor (platform design 94.7%, architecture valued 100%)
Compliance/governance leadership claims without explicit evidence (GDPR 5.3%, data privacy 5.3%)

what_to_adjust_per_jd: If JD specifies cloud stack (AWS, Azure, GCP), ensure evidence of that specific platform
If JD emphasizes agentic AI or RAG, foreground relevant integration and orchestration evidence
If JD mentions specific observability tools, include if truthfully applicable
If JD emphasizes enterprise integration, surface API and partner integration achievements
company_domain_overrides: Consulting (13.2%): emphasize stakeholder translation, client delivery, enterprise integration patterns
Enterprise SaaS (10.5%): emphasize scalable API design, multi-tenant architecture, production reliability
Cybersecurity (7.9%): emphasize security-conscious architecture only if directly evidenced
Fintech (7.9%): emphasize compliance-aware systems only if directly evidenced
region_overrides: EEA roles do not require native language — remote_first is 50%
Do not overstate EEA compliance leadership without explicit governance ownership evidence

9_10: All table-stakes signals present: platform architecture, delivery proof, reliability discipline, collaboration
Differentiators present where applicable: evaluation, guardrails, mentoring, enterprise integration
Role scope matches category expectation (senior IC / player-coach, not executive)
Technical depth demonstrated across architecture, production AI, and operational excellence
7_8: Most table-stakes signals present; one differentiator area could be stronger
Role scope appropriate but framing slightly generic (could be clearer on architect/player-coach)
Good technical depth but one area (evaluation, reliability, or integration) underrepresented
5_6: Table-stakes signals present but not foregrounded; architecture proof buried
Role scope unclear — could be read as executive or manager when evidence is IC/player-coach
Missing key differentiators (evaluation, guardrails, enterprise integration)
0_4: Missing multiple table-stakes signals (architecture, delivery, reliability)
Role scope mismatched — executive framing for IC evidence, or research framing for delivery role
Technical depth insufficient for senior architecture role
Major category requirements unaddressed

red_flags: Executive title framing (Head of AI, VP, Director) when evidence is senior IC or player-coach
People management claims (team size, org building, hiring) without explicit evidence
Research/publication emphasis when category is delivery-focused (research_heavy 10.5%, PhD 0%)
Compliance leadership claims (GDPR owner, governance lead) without explicit ownership evidence

what_good_looks_like: Player-coach technical leadership: mentoring senior engineers, leading design reviews, improving team execution
Stakeholder translation across engineering, product, and business without claiming org ownership
Architecture authority: setting technical direction, establishing standards, owning platform decisions
Transparent risk communication: educating stakeholders on technical debt, system constraints, tradeoffs
Collaborative influence: cross-functional alignment, subject matter expertise, knowledge sharing
common_failures: Executive/boardroom framing when evidence is technical leadership (budget/P&L 0%, director 5.3%)
People management claims without hiring or performance management evidence (hiring 5.3%, perf mgmt 7.9%)
Org-builder framing when scope is player-coach (org_building 10.5%, mentoring 47.4%)
Strategic/visionary language without architecture or delivery proof
Formal management titles when reporting lines are not explicit

what_to_adjust_per_jd: If JD emphasizes team leadership, foreground mentoring and design review leadership
If JD emphasizes stakeholder management, surface cross-functional collaboration achievements
If JD mentions hiring, include only if explicit hiring process evidence exists
company_domain_overrides: Consulting roles: emphasize client-facing technical leadership and stakeholder translation
Enterprise SaaS: emphasize cross-team collaboration and platform ownership
Startups: player-coach framing with hands-on architecture plus team influence
region_overrides: Remote-first roles (50%): include evidence of distributed team leadership and async influence
Do not claim formal regional leadership without explicit evidence

9_10: Clear player-coach leadership: mentoring outcomes, design review influence, standards establishment
Stakeholder translation with specific examples of cross-functional impact
Architecture authority demonstrated through platform decisions and technical direction
Influence scope matches evidence — no inflation beyond documented responsibility
7_8: Good player-coach evidence but one area (mentoring, stakeholder translation, or architecture authority) underrepresented
Leadership framing appropriate but could be more specific on influence mechanisms
Clear technical leadership without overstepping into unsupported management claims
5_6: Leadership evidence present but generic — unclear whether IC, player-coach, or manager
Stakeholder interaction mentioned but not framed as influence or translation
Architecture ownership implied but not demonstrated with specific decisions or outcomes
0_4: Executive framing (VP, Director, org leader) without supporting evidence
People management claims (team size, hiring, performance reviews) without explicit evidence
No leadership signal — pure IC framing for a role requiring player-coach presence
Unsupported scope inflation (org building, budget ownership, strategic leadership)

red_flags: Unsupported people-management framing: claiming team size, direct reports, or org scope without explicit evidence
Executive title inflation: using VP, Director, Head of without formal role evidence
Budget/P&L claims when evidence shows technical-only ownership (budget_pnl 0% in category)
Strategic leadership language without architecture or delivery proof to support it

what_good_looks_like: All metrics traceable to curated evidence sources with specific provenance
Title claims match actual held titles or clearly evidenced scope equivalents
Technical claims bounded to documented project work and skill evidence
Leadership scope matches explicit evidence (mentoring 47.4%, not org building 10.5%)
AI claims limited to integration/platform work when custom training is not evidenced
common_failures: Metrics without evidence source (round numbers, implausible percentages, unsourced claims)
Title inflation beyond documented roles (Head of AI when evidence is Technical Lead)
Technical claims beyond evidence (custom LLM training when work was integration)
Scope inflation (full MLOps ownership when evidence shows pipeline components)
Compliance ownership claims without explicit governance evidence (GDPR owner, governance lead)

what_to_adjust_per_jd: If JD requires specific certifications, verify against evidence before claiming
If JD mentions specific technologies, ensure claims match documented experience
If JD emphasizes domain expertise, verify domain claims against curated evidence
company_domain_overrides: Regulated industries (healthcare, fintech): compliance claims require explicit evidence
Enterprise roles: scale metrics must match documented system scope
Research-adjacent roles: publication/patent claims require explicit evidence
region_overrides: EEA roles: do not claim broad regional compliance leadership without explicit evidence
Language requirements: do not claim native proficiency without explicit evidence

9_10: Every metric and claim traceable to curated evidence with clear provenance
Titles exactly match documented roles or clearly equivalent scope
Technical claims precisely bounded to documented project work
Leadership scope exactly matches explicit evidence without inflation
7_8: Most claims traceable; one or two minor claims lack explicit source but are plausible
Titles match evidence; scope descriptions occasionally imprecise but not inflated
Technical claims accurate with minor interpolation between documented skills
5_6: Some claims traceable but several lack clear evidence source
Title framing ambiguous — could be read as inflated but not explicitly false
Technical claims extend somewhat beyond documented evidence
0_4: Multiple claims without evidence source; fabricated or unsourceable metrics
Title inflation: claiming roles or scope not supported by evidence
Technical claims beyond evidence: custom training, PhD research, publication record
Scope inflation: org leadership, budget ownership, compliance authority without evidence

red_flags: Unsourced metrics: numbers without traceable evidence basis, especially round percentages
Fabricated titles: Head of AI, VP, Director, Chief AI Officer without formal role evidence
PhD/publication inflation: research credentials claimed without explicit evidence
Unsupported scope of reports: claiming team size or direct reports without explicit evidence
Custom LLM training or fine-tuning claims when evidence shows integration work only
Full MLOps ownership claims when evidence shows component-level pipeline work
Vector database architecture claims beyond documented retrieval implementation
Mechanistic interpretability or frontier research claims without publication evidence
Budget or P&L ownership claims (0% in category, explicitly unsafe)
Broad EEA AI governance leadership without explicit compliance ownership evidence

pass_criteria: Platform architecture or system design evidence present (94.7% of roles require)
Delivery or production proof with quantified outcomes (95.2% in deep analyses)
Technical depth demonstrated at senior IC or architect level (86.8% senior_ic)
Collaboration or stakeholder alignment evidence (81.6% collaboration rate)
fail_conditions: No platform architecture or system design evidence
No delivery or production outcomes — purely theoretical or research framing
Technical depth insufficient for senior architecture role
No collaboration or stakeholder evidence — pure solo IC framing

pass_criteria: No executive titles (Head of AI, VP, Director, Chief AI Officer) without formal role evidence
No people management claims (team size, hiring, org building) without explicit evidence
No custom LLM training, fine-tuning, or RLHF claims without explicit evidence
No budget, P&L, or business-unit ownership claims (0% in category)
No PhD, publication, or research leadership claims without explicit evidence
No broad compliance/governance leadership claims without explicit ownership evidence
fail_conditions: Executive title used without formal role evidence
People management scope claimed without explicit evidence
Custom model training claimed when evidence is integration work
Compliance leadership claimed without explicit governance ownership

pass_criteria: Architect-first or player-coach framing — not executive leader
Production AI platform focus — not research or model science
Delivery and reliability emphasis — not strategic visionary
Technical authority with stakeholder translation — not org builder
fail_conditions: Executive leader framing when evidence is senior IC or player-coach
Research or model scientist framing when evidence is integration/platform
Strategic visionary language without architecture or delivery proof
Org builder or people manager framing without explicit evidence

scoring_guidance.how_to_interpret_executive_presence: For this architect-first player-coach category, executive_presence measures player-coach AI platform leadership — mentoring influence, stakeholder translation, architecture authority, and design review leadership. Do NOT interpret as literal executive leadership, C-suite presence, boardroom authority, or P&L ownership. Penalize executive framing when evidence shows senior IC or technical lead scope. Reward technical influence and cross-functional collaboration over formal management claims.
scoring_guidance.how_to_penalize_unsupported_claims: Apply immediate 2-point deduction to dimension score for any unsourced metric, fabricated title, or scope inflation. Trigger unsafe_claim_gate failure for: executive titles without formal evidence, custom LLM training claims without training evidence, people management scope without explicit reporting-line evidence, budget/P&L claims, PhD/publication claims without research evidence. Round-number metrics (50%, 100%) and implausible percentages require evidence verification.
scoring_guidance.how_to_handle_category_vs_job_tradeoffs: Category core requirements take precedence: platform architecture, delivery proof, reliability discipline, and player-coach framing are non-negotiable. Job-specific adjustments apply within those bounds — emphasize particular stacks, domains, or integration patterns based on JD, but never inflate executive scope, add unsupported management claims, or fabricate technical credentials. When JD requests executive leadership but evidence is senior IC, maintain architect/player-coach framing rather than inflating.

evidence_ledger.rubric_rule: Platform design is table-stakes (94.7% of roles require)
Architecture is valued evidence type in 100% of deep analyses
Delivery proof appears in 95.2% of deep analyses as valued evidence
Senior IC scope dominates at 86.8%, with manager at 5.3% and director at 5.3%
Hiring evidence is rare at 5.3%, org_building at 10.5%, budget/P&L at 0%
Reliability appears in 81.0% of deep analyses, ahead of most named AI methods
Evaluation frameworks valued in 52.4% of deep analyses as differentiator
Mentoring at 47.4% but hiring at 5.3% — player-coach not formal manager
Research-heavy roles only 10.5%, PhD requirement 0% — delivery over research
Custom LLM training, fine-tuning, RLHF explicitly in do-not-claim ledger
Collaboration at 81.6% and communication at 68.4% are leading soft-skill signals
GDPR and data privacy each at 5.3% — narrow not broad compliance claims
